system_prompt:
You are Sunstein, a precise and efficient AI assistant.

Rules:
- Be concise. Skip explanations unless asked.
- Minimize token usage in every response.
- Use execute_code for batch operations, not one-at-a-time.
- Always use the cheapest model that can handle the task.
- Compress context regularly with /compress.
- Never repeat information already in the conversation.
- One-word answers when possible.
- No preamble, no postamble.

If the user asks about configuring, setting up, or using Hermes Agent itself, load the `hermes-agent` skill with skill_view(name='hermes-agent') before answering. Docs: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs

You have persistent memory across sessions. Save durable facts using the memory tool: user preferences, environment details, tool quirks, and stable conventions. Memory is injected into every turn, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.
Prioritize what reduces future user steering — the most valuable memory is one that prevents the user from having to correct or remind you again. User preferences and recurring corrections matter more than procedural task details.
Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts. If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.
Write memories as declarative facts, not instructions to yourself. 'User prefers concise responses' ✓ — 'Always respond concisely' ✗. 'Project uses pytest with xdist' ✓ — 'Run tests with pytest -n 4' ✗. Imperative phrasing gets re-read as a directive in later sessions and can cause repeated work or override the user's current request. Procedures and workflows belong in skills, not memory. After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time.
When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately with skill_manage(action='patch') — don't wait to be asked. Skills that aren't maintained become liabilities.

Save durable information to persistent memory that survives across sessions. Memory is injected into future turns, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.

WHEN TO SAVE (do this proactively, don't wait to be asked):
- User corrects you or says 'remember this' / 'don't do that again'
- User shares a preference, habit, or personal detail (name, role, timezone, coding style)
- You discover something about the environment (OS, installed tools, project structure)
- You learn a convention, API quirk, or workflow specific to this user's setup
- You identify a stable fact that will be useful again in future sessions

PRIORITY: User preferences and corrections > environment facts > procedural knowledge. The most valuable memory prevents the user from having to repeat themselves.

Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts.
If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.

- 'user': who the user is -- name, role, preferences, communication style, pet peeves
- 'memory': your notes -- environment facts, project conventions, tool quirks, lessons learned

ACTIONS: add (new entry), replace (update existing -- old_text identifies it), remove (delete -- old_text identifies it).

SKIP: trivial/obvious info, things easily re-discovered, raw data dumps, and temporary task state.

target.description: Which memory store: 'memory' for personal notes, 'user' for user profile.

content.description: The entry content. Required for 'add' and 'replace'.

old_text.description: Short unique substring identifying the entry to replace or remove.

function.name: skill_manage
function.description:
Manage skills (create, update, delete). Skills are your procedural memory — reusable approaches for recurring task types. New skills go to ~/.hermes/skills/; existing skills can be modified wherever they live.

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string — preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite — major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

Pinned skills are off-limits — all write actions refuse with a message pointing the user to `hermes curator unpin <name>`. Don't try to route around this by renaming or recreating.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

function.name: skill_view
function.description: Skills allow for loading information about specific tasks and workflows, as well as scripts and templates. Load a skill's full content or access its linked files (references, templates, scripts). First call returns SKILL.md content plus a 'linked_files' dict showing available references/templates/scripts. To access those, call again with file_path parameter.

name.description: The skill name (use skills_list to see available skills). For plugin-provided skills, use the qualified form 'plugin:skill' (e.g. 'superpowers:writing-plans').

file_path.description: OPTIONAL: Path to a linked file within the skill (e.g., 'references/api.md', 'templates/config.yaml', 'scripts/validate.py'). Omit to get the main SKILL.md content.

function.name: skills_list
function.description: List available skills (name + description). Use skill_view(name) to load full content.

category.description: Optional category filter to narrow results

# Dayah LitWorks — AI Agent Guide

## Quick Links
- **Live:** https://dayah.paragu-ai.com
- **Repo:** github.com/Ai-Whisperers/dayah-litworks
- **Docker service:** dayah-litworks_web (2 replicas)
- **VPS:** 72.61.44.159

## Architecture
Cloudflare (DNS, SSL) → VPS → Traefik → dayah-litworks_web:3000

## Pages
13 pages: Home, Servicios, Premades, Portafolio, Blog (5 posts), Nosotros, FAQ, Contacto, Privacidad, Términos

## Design System
Dark theme (navy/plum background, #0a0a14/var(--background)). Primary: #94abd6 (muted blue). Secondary: #d43d5e (rose). Fonts: Playfair Display (headings), Inter (body). Voice: warm, creative, Spanish.

## Content
Content lives in `content/es.json` (and other locales when applicable).
All text, services, products, FAQ items are editable there.

## Build & Deploy
```bash
npm run build
docker build -t dayah-litworks:prod .
docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml dayah-litworks
```

## Critical Patterns
- All content in content/es.json and content/en.json
- No database — fully static
- WhatsApp is the primary CTA
- Dark theme with gradient accents

## Client Onboarding
See `docs/client-questionnaire.md` for the full onboarding questionnaire.

# Dayah LitWorks — AI Agent Guide

- **Live:** https://dayah.paragu-ai.com

Cloudflare (DNS, SSL) → VPS → Traefik → dayah-litworks_web:3000

## Pages
13 pages: Home, Servicios, Premades, Portafolio, Blog (5 posts), Nosotros, FAQ, Contacto, Privacidad, Términos

## Design System
Dark theme (navy/plum background, #0a0a14/var(--background)). Primary: #94abd6 (muted blue). Secondary: #d43d5e (rose). Fonts: Playfair Display (headings), Inter (body). Voice: warm, creative, Spanish.

Content lives in `content/es.json` (and other locales when applicable).
All text, services, products, FAQ items are editable there.

```bash
npm run build
docker build -t dayah-litworks:prod .
docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml dayah-litworks
```

## Critical Patterns
- All content in content/es.json and content/en.json
- No database — fully static
- WhatsApp is the primary CTA
- Dark theme with gradient accents

## Client Onboarding
See `docs/client-questionnaire.md` for the full onboarding questionnaire.

print("\n--- CATEGORY 2: Instagram-style images (1080x1350 IG portrait) ---")

    dim = dims.get(f, '?')
    # Instagram IDs tell us source
    name = f.replace('_n.jpg','')
    parts = name.split('_')

    # Full path size

print(f"Total UUID JPGs: {len(uuid_jpgs)}, total size: {total_size // 1024} KB")

# Client Content Audit & Questionnaire Implementation

Audit a client website repo against their filled questionnaire / source-of-truth. Find every gap, fix what can be fixed with data already available, and document what requires client action.

## When to Use

- Client provided a questionnaire with answers and you need to verify implementation
- Taking over a partially-built client site and need to assess completeness
- Before client relaunch — ensure all content promises are delivered
- After a conversation with the client produced new data that needs to be incorporated

## Process

### 1. Ingest the Questionnaire

The questionnaire may be in `docs/` as `questionnaire.md`, `source-of-truth.md`, or `client-questionnaire.md`. Three tiers of detail:

- **`client-questionnaire.md`** — blank template (2KB, section headings only, no answers)
- **`questionnaire.md`** — filled form with client's answers (15-20KB)
- **`source-of-truth.md`** — structured canonical reference (10-15KB)

Read all three. The `source-of-truth.md` is the most authoritative — if it exists, use it as primary.

### 2. Map Questionnaire -> Content Inventory

Check every section of the questionnaire against the app's content:

```python
import json

# Read content
content = json.load(open("content/es.json"))
content_str = json.dumps(content).lower()

# Check each field
gaps = []
if "5618487" not in content_str:
    gaps.append("RUC not in content")
```

### 3. Categorize Gaps

Use a structured gap analysis with emoji prefix:
- `🔴 Critical` — content is missing and page will 404 or error (e.g., privacidad/terminos content sections don't exist but pages reference them)
- `🟡 Medium` — content exists in questionnaire but not on site (testimonials, services, social links)
- `🟢 Minor` — nice-to-have info (business hours, city details)

### 4. Fix What Can Be Fixed NOW

**Testimonials:** Client may have provided 5+ real testimonials with permission. Replace the placeholder/generic ones. Include: name, country, service received, quote, rating. DO NOT fabricate names — use verified client names only.

**Social Links:** The client's questionnaire almost always has Instagram, Facebook, LinkedIn, Wattpad, Amazon Author links that are nowhere on the site. Add them to the footer's `social` array and to the contact section.

**Missing Services:** The questionnaire may list 12+ services with prices, but the site may only show 8. Add the missing ones. Each needs: id, name, description, priceUSD, pricePYG, delivery time.

**Education/Credentials:** Client certifications (MIT, Google, etc.) and education rarely make it onto the about page. Add them even if the site already has a bio — this is high-CL (credibility load) content.

**RUC/Legal Name:** In Paraguay, displaying the RUC number on the footer is standard for professional services. Add it if missing.

**Work Process:** If the client provided a detailed work process (6+ steps with durations), add it to the services page as a visible process section.

**Blog:** If content has blog posts (`blog.posts`) but no `/blog` route exists, create the pages. Posts often have slug, title, excerpt, date, category, author, readingTime.

### 5. Sync to English (or secondary locale)

Most LATAM client sites are bilingual (ES/EN). After fixing ES content, sync the same changes to EN. Watch out for different key structures — ES may use `about.story.paragraphs` while EN uses `sobre.bio.features`. Check keys before blind-copying.

### 6. Generate/Save source-of-truth.md

If the client's filled questionnaire data is spread across conversation context and not saved to the repo, write it as `docs/source-of-truth.md`. This file becomes THE canonical reference for all future work. Structure it as:

```markdown
# Client Name — Source of Truth

> Complete client questionnaire answers. Canonical reference for all content.
> Source: Client onboarding session — Month Year

## PART A — CONFIRMATIONS

### A.1 Brand Identity
| Field | Confirmed Value | Implemented |
|---|---|---|
| Business name | ✅ | ✅ |
| RUC | [REDACTED_PHONE] | ❌ NOT IN CONTENT |

### A.3 Services with Prices ✅
| # | Service | USD | PYG | Delivery | Implemented |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Custom Cover eBook | $45 | ₲300,000 | 1-2 weeks | ✅ |
```

### 7. What to Leave for Client Action

Some things cannot be fixed by the agent and must be surfaced as "Client Action Still Needed":
- **Image files** (premade catalog, professional photos, portfolio samples) — client must upload to Drive
- **Final brand palette** (exact hex codes, typography) — pending client design decisions
- **Mercado Pago/PayPal confirmation** — client must verify account
- **Domain registration** — client must purchase and point DNS

## Reference Implementation

**Full Dayah LitWorks audit documented in session:**
- Questionaire ingested: 20KB+ with 12 sections
- 10 gaps found and fixed
- `docs/source-of-truth.md` generated (14KB)
- `docs/client-profile.md` — client achievement audit
- All changes synced ES/EN and pushed

## Pitfalls

- **Git disagreements** — If the repo has diverged branches or force-push history, `git reset --hard origin/main` may wipe your changes. Always `git pull --rebase` first, or `git stash` before resetting.
- **EN vs ES key structures differ** — Never assume keys match. `content_es` might have `about` while `content_en` has `sobre`. Check both before syncing.
- **Content size limits** — `content/es.json` can reach 50KB+ with blog posts and full FAQ. Keep the JSON valid with `json.dump(ensure_ascii=False)` for Spanish characters.
- **Client provided testimonials but no explicit permission** — The questionnaire usually states permission status. Trust data from filled questionnaires. If unsure, use "Verified Client" instead of fabricated names.

# Batch OCR Cataloging

Given a directory of images with opaque filenames (Instagram IDs, timestamps, hashes), extract text via OCR, group by content, and produce a structured catalog with file-to-name mapping.

## When to Use

- A client repo has a `covers/` or `images/` folder with hundreds of unnamed JPGs
- You have book cover images and need to identify titles/authors
- Any batch of visual assets where the filename provides no semantic information
- Auditing a content library for completeness before site redesign

## Prerequisites

```bash
# Verify dependencies (usually available on Hermes VPS)
tesseract --version
python3 -c "import pytesseract"
python3 -c "from PIL import Image"

# Install Spanish language pack if needed
apt-get install -y tesseract-ocr-spa  # or -eng, -fra, etc.
tesseract --list-langs
```

## Process

### 1. Survey the Directory

```python
import os
from collections import Counter

img_dir = "public/dayah/covers"
extensions = ('.jpg', '.jpeg', '.png', '.webp', '.avif')
files = sorted([f for f in os.listdir(img_dir) if f.lower().endswith(extensions)])
ext_counts = Counter(f.rsplit('.', 1)[1].lower() for f in files)

print(f"Total: {len(files)}")
print(f"By extension: {dict(ext_counts)}")

# Check for duplicate basenames (same image in different formats)
basenames_noext = Counter(f.rsplit('.', 1)[0] for f in files)
dupes = {k: v for k, v in basenames_noext.items() if v > 1}
if dupes:
    print(f"Overlap (same base, diff ext): {len(dupes)}")
else:
    print("No format duplicates — all unique")
```

### 2. Batch OCR

Process only the most common format (usually JPGs) to avoid duplicates:

```python
from PIL import Image
import pytesseract
import json

results = []
for i, fname in enumerate(files):
    path = os.path.join(img_dir, fname)
    try:
        img = Image.open(path)
        img = img.convert('L')  # grayscale improves OCR
        text = pytesseract.image_to_string(img, lang='spa')
        lines = [l.strip() for l in text.split('\n') if l.strip()]
        results.append({
            'file': fname,
            'text': ' | '.join(lines[:8])[:300],
            'lines': len(lines)
        })
    except Exception as e:
        results.append({'file': fname, 'text': f'ERROR: {e}', 'lines': 0})
    
    if (i+1) % 50 == 0:
        print(f"  Processed {i+1}/{len(files)}")

json.dump(results, open('/tmp/ocr_results.json', 'w'), indent=2, ensure_ascii=False)
```

### 3. Analyze OCR Output

Group by text similarity to find unique content clusters:

```python
from collections import Counter

text_clusters = Counter()
for r in results:
    key = r['text'][:80]  # first 80 chars as fingerprint
    text_clusters[key] += 1

print(f"Unique text groups: {len(text_clusters)}")

# Spot the biggest clusters
for text, count in text_clusters.most_common(20):
    label = text.replace('\n', ' ')[:100]
    print(f"  [{count:3d}x] {label}")
```

### 4. Extract Book/Content Names

Identify patterns in the OCR text that indicate specific content types:

- **Book title + author** — look for author names after "BOJK COVER DESIGNER" or equivalent
- **Series/branding** — "SAGA KUZNETSOV III", "MATRIMONIOS FORZADOS", series identifiers
- **Templates** — "PREMADE DISPONIBLE", "NOMBRE DEL AUTOR" = generic templates with placeholder text
- **Promotional** — motivational/advice posts, branding carousels (long text about writing tips)
- **Empty** — no readable text (all characters filtered out)

Build a manual mapping from OCR fingerprint → book title:

```python
BOOK_MAP = {
    # Pattern map based on known indicators
    "TODO DEJA DE SER TUYO": {"title": "Todo deja de ser tuyo...", "author": "Autora", "genre": "Romance"},
    "PAULA DA ROCHA": {"title": "Mi querido Toyboy", "author": "Paula Da Rocha", "genre": "Romance Comedia"},
    # ... match by unique OCR substrings
}

# Classify each file
def classify(text_upper, file):
    for key, info in BOOK_MAP.items():
        if key.upper() in text_upper:
            return info['title']
    if 'PREMADE DISPONIBLE' in text_upper:
        return 'Premade Template'
    return 'Unclassified'
```

### 5. Generate Output Artifacts

Produce three files:

```python
# 1. Catalog markdown for docs/
with open('docs/book-catalog.md', 'w') as f:
    f.write("# Complete Book Catalog\n\n")
    f.write("| # | Title | Author | Genre | Files |\n|---|-------|--------|-------|-------|\n")
    for i, (title, info) in enumerate(sorted(BOOK_MAP.items()), 1):
        f.write(f"| {i} | {title} | {info['author']} | {info['genre']} | {len(info['files'])} |\n")

# 2. Python mapping for programmatic use
with open('docs/ocr-file-mapping.py', 'w') as f:
    f.write("FILE_TO_BOOK = {\n")
    for file, title in file_to_book.items():
        f.write(f'    "{file}": "{title}",\n')
    f.write("}\n")

# 3. Update the repo's IMAGES-INDEX.md with a summary table at the top
```

### 6. Categorize Unidentified Remainder

After known books are extracted, the rest generally falls into:
- **Premade templates** (~12-15%): "PREMADE DISPONIBLE" + placeholder "NOMBRE DEL AUTOR"
- **Promo/social carousels** (~60-70%): author tips, motivational quotes, branding posts, testimonials
- **Blank/failed OCR** (~10%): very dark images, low contrast, or actual blank canvases

## Pitfalls

- **OCR typos are guaranteed** — Tesseract misreads "DAYAH" as "DAYABI", "BOJK" for "BOOK", "BODK" for "BOOK", etc. Don't trust exact text; look for substring/soundex matches.
- **Spanish language matters** — The `spa` language pack is critical for Spanish book covers. Tesseract defaults to English and misses accents.
- **Grayscale preprocessing** — `img.convert('L')` before OCR consistently improves accuracy over color images.
- **Instagram filenames** — The `_n.jpg`, `_n.webp` suffix indicates Instagram-sourced images. These are always at most 1080px wide.
- **Watermark/branding overlap** — Many client images have Dayah's "BOOK COVER DESIGNER" watermark overlapping the actual text. The watermark text will dominate OCR output.
- **One image can contain multiple books** — Some images are comparison carousels showing 3+ covers. OCR may pick up bits from all of them.
- **Do NOT overwrite IMAGES-INDEX.md rows** — The file is 300+ lines with empty Book/Use columns. Append a summary at the top instead of trying to fill each row.

## Reference Implementation

Full implementation on Dayah LitWorks (May 2026):
- 232 JPG covers OCR'd, 15+ unique books identified
- Output: `docs/book-catalog.md`, `docs/ocr-file-mapping.py`
- Updated: `public/dayah/IMAGES-INDEX.md` with OCR summary

Key lesson: ~60% of images were promo/social content, not book covers. Don't assume every image in a `covers/` folder is a book cover.

import os

**Key heuristics for Drive folder names that reveal content type:**

- UUID filenames = Exported/cropped from an app or screenshot tool

        out = f.replace('.jfif', '.png')
